ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general snail food product class (FOODON_03315595); no specific multi-preparation Vietnamese snail platter class exists. Closest parent class used as the defensible ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers snails and gastropods as a food category; no specific code for a multi-style Vietnamese snail platter exists. Closest parent code used as the defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical code applicable for this prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One sharing platter of approximately 500g, intended for 2–4 people, featuring five different snail preparations served together.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Snails, raw (FDC ID 175176) and cooked seafood composites; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for regional seasoning adjustments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for a five-preparation Vietnamese snail platter exists in any approved source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central snail (raw and cooked) data (FDC ID 175176) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional seasoning and cooking-method adjustments. Input record values (energy 150 kcal, protein 12g, fat 9g, carbohydrate 5g, sodium 360mg) are broadly consistent with USDA snail data adjusted for added fats, fish sauce, and coconut milk. Values retained from input with source upgraded to approved references. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamins A/B-group, vitamin D/E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1 as detailed breakdown not available in USDA or FAO/INFOODS for this composite preparation.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous estimate based on shellfish/seafood with low carbohydrate content; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese multi-preparation snail platter. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on analogous shellfish and low-carbohydrate seafood dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 5g/100g; glycemic load per full 500g serving = (25 × 25.0) / 100 = 6.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360mg/100g from fish sauce; estimated 1800mg sodium per 500g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced fish sauce during preparation, as a full 500g serving delivers approximately 1800mg sodium — approaching the 2000mg daily limit in a single dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5g/100g) and low sugar (1.6g/100g); high protein (12g/100g) moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content of 5g/100g and high protein from snails which slows gastric emptying; however, if served alongside rice or bread, total carbohydrate load must be accounted for in the meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0g/100g from coconut milk and butter; sodium 360mg/100g; cholesterol 50mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to the combined sodium (360mg/100g) and saturated fat (2.0g/100g) load from fish sauce, coconut milk, and butter; requesting substitution of butter with plant oil and reduced fish sauce will meaningfully lower cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory herbs (ginger, lemongrass); low saturated fat (2.0g/100g); nightshade ingredients present (chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and lemongrass provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; however, RA-sensitive individuals should monitor symptoms related to nightshade ingredients (chilli) and request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 5 Snail in 1 (Ốc Mec Thiên Long) is a Vietnamese specialty dish featuring five different preparations of snails served together as a sharing platter, typically weighing approximately 500g and intended for 2–4 diners. The dish showcases the Vietnamese culinary tradition of ốc (snail) cookery, presenting snails prepared through stir-frying, grilling, steaming, stewing, and braising in a single communal serving. Key aromatics include lemongrass, ginger, chilli, Vietnamese coriander, basil, and mint, while the sauce base typically incorporates fish sauce, coconut milk, butter, peanuts, and lime juice, creating a complex interplay of savoury, spicy, herbal, and slightly sweet flavour profiles. The texture varies across preparations — grilled snails offer a smoky, firm bite; steamed snails are tender and delicate; stir-fried preparations carry a caramelised, umami-rich coating. Nutritionally, the dish provides 150 kcal per 100g, with a high protein content of 12g/100g from the snail meat, moderate fat at 9g/100g (saturated fat 2.0g/100g from coconut milk and butter), and low carbohydrate at 5g/100g. Sodium is elevated at 360mg/100g due to fish sauce, making this dish a concern for hypertension management. The glycaemic index is estimated as low (approximately 25), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments. The dish is a popular street food and restaurant offering in Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Vietnam, often served with fresh herbs, lime wedges, and chilli dipping sauce. Known in Chinese as 五味田螺, in Malay as Siput Lima Cara, and in Tamil as ஐந்து வகை நத்தை.
common_user_queries: What is Oc Mec Thien Long?
How many calories in a snail platter?
Is snail dish suitable for diabetics?
Is Oc Mec high in sodium?
What are the five snail preparations in this dish?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for a five-preparation Vietnamese snail platter exists in any approved primary nutrient database. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central snail data (FDC ID 175176) with FAO/INFOODS SEA adjustments for added fats, fish sauce, and coconut milk. Input record values are broadly consistent with this adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin breakdown, fat-soluble vitamins, minerals, and moisture data for this composite five-preparation Vietnamese snail platter are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables as of 2026. Core macros (protein_g 12.0, fat_g 9.0, carbohydrate_g 5.0) are non-zero and consistent with USDA snail data adjusted for cooking fats and seasoning. trans_fat_g is 0.1g/100g (trace level from butter). Input record had two duplicate portion entries both labelled with 500g and is_default true; deduplicated to single 500g sharing platter serving. Country of origin in input was listed as Singapore, reflecting the serving context; region of origin noted as Vietnam (served in Singapore). Halal status is uncertain as fish sauce and snail sourcing are not certified; is_halal set to false pending verification. Multilingual local names added for Singaporean serving context: Chinese Simplified/Traditional 五味田螺, Malay Siput Lima Cara, Tamil ஐந்து வகை நத்தை, Hindi पाँच तरह के घोंघे. Ontology match types corrected to closest_parent (allowed value) for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact code exists for this composite dish.
